seed_triplets_accentMap.interaction: Triplets with one accent per bar — the seed rolls at three notes per beat, and one note gets a punchy highlight per bar. The accent rotates through seed-positions as the pitches evolve, creating a natural spotlight on whichever note happens to occupy the accent slot.
steps: Feel the triplet pulse, one seed statement per beat.
Pick the accent position (e.g., beat 1, first note of the seed).
State the seed twice identically, accent consistent.
Change one note per beat; the accent stays STRUCTURAL.
Hear how the accented pitch changes identity as the seed drifts.
examples: Triplets: ROOT-third-fifth (root accented). Next: ROOT-third-SIXTH. Next: ROOT-FOURTH-sixth.
Triplets: second-fourth-SIXTH (sixth accented). Next: THIRD-fourth-SIXTH. Next: third-FIFTH-SIXTH.
seed_triplets_accentMap.watchOut: Common trap: accenting the note you just changed to 'show it off'. No — the accent position is structural; the evolving seed is what makes different notes land in that slot.

seed_triplets_forte.interaction: Full-volume rolling triplets — the seed statements tumble out at three notes per beat, every one at maximum intensity. Each beat holds a complete pitch seed, and one note changes per beat. It's the most kinetic form of the constraint: rolling, loud, relentlessly evolving.
steps: Lock the triplet pulse at full volume.
State the seed twice identically to establish it.
Change one note per beat — commit fully to every new pitch.
Don't let the volume drag the triplets into duple feel.
Aim for 10-14 beats of evolving seed statements.
examples: Loud triplets: root-third-fifth → root-third-SEVENTH → root-SECOND-seventh → FOURTH-second-seventh.
Loud triplets: fifth-sixth-seventh → fifth-sixth-SECOND → fifth-ROOT-second → FOURTH-root-second.
seed_triplets_forte.watchOut: Common trap: at full volume the triplet feel collapses into straight eighths. Feel the three-per-beat swing in your body before you commit to the volume.

forbidden_river_swell.interaction: With a shrunk palette flowing in even quarters, you have nowhere to hide melodically — the swell becomes the storyteller. Volume shape has to do the work that pitch variety normally would, so every crescendo and decay gives the limited notes fresh meaning.
steps: Look at your allowed palette first and mentally bracket the forbidden notes as if they simply don't exist on your instrument.
Lock in steady quarters — no rushing, no gaps — and let the evenness become a canvas.
Begin the swell from near-silence, push toward full volume around the midpoint, and release back down by the phrase end.
Reuse the same 2-3 notes intentionally during the softest sections — repetition reads as focus, not laziness, when dynamics are moving.
Change which allowed note you emphasize at each dynamic peak so the swell lands on a different palette color each time.
examples: lowest allowed → second allowed → third allowed → second allowed, looping in quarters while the volume climbs and fades
highest allowed repeated four times quietly → step down through the palette at full volume → return to the highest for the decay
forbidden_river_swell.watchOut: The biggest trap is your fingers or voice reflexively sliding toward a forbidden note during the loud peak — that's exactly when habit overrides attention. If you catch one leaking, drop volume immediately and restart the swell.

forbidden_river_terraces.interaction: Even quarters plus hard volume switches plus a reduced palette — each terrace becomes a new room built from the same few bricks. The rhythm stays flat so the ear tracks pitch repetition against sudden dynamic shifts, turning a small vocabulary into strong architecture.
steps: Identify your allowed notes and pick a 'quiet-zone' subset (maybe the lower half) and a 'loud-zone' subset (the upper half).
Hold steady quarter-note motion throughout — no rushing between terraces.
Switch dynamic levels cleanly at bar lines, no fade between them.
When you jump to a new terrace, jump to a different allowed note too, so the dynamic change and the pitch change reinforce each other.
Use at least three distinct volume levels across the round — not just loud and soft.
examples: quiet bar: second allowed → second allowed → third allowed → second allowed, then loud bar: highest allowed → second-highest → highest → second-highest
medium bar cycling the lowest two allowed, then sudden forte bar cycling the top two allowed, then sudden pianissimo bar on a single middle allowed note
forbidden_river_terraces.watchOut: Cheating the dynamic edge by crescendoing into the next terrace. The switch must be a wall, not a ramp. If you hear any fade, you're smoothing instead of terracing.

forbidden_river_whisper.interaction: Quiet flowing quarters with a reduced palette is a meditation — almost nothing to hold onto but the turning of the allowed notes. The whisper forces precision, the river forces continuity, and the missing notes force you to find beauty in what's left.
steps: Set your volume to the softest you can reliably sustain — if it cracks, back off slightly.
Keep the quarter-note pulse unshakeable; the quieter you are, the easier it is to rush.
Let your allowed notes cycle slowly; don't feel obligated to use all of them in every bar.
Listen for the tiny tonal differences between your palette notes — at whisper volume, small pitch changes feel big.
Breathe or relax between phrases even though the notes keep flowing.
examples: two bars of just the lowest two allowed notes alternating, then two bars adding a third allowed note above
a gentle four-note cycle through the palette repeated three times, each pass a shade quieter than the last
forbidden_river_whisper.watchOut: Getting loud without noticing. Whisper drifts upward under concentration. If you feel the palette 'opening up' in your chest, you've left whisper territory.

forbidden_river_accentMap.interaction: With an even quarter flow and a shrunk palette, the one loud note per bar becomes a spotlight you choose carefully. You're deciding which of the few allowed notes deserves the accent, and the repetition of the quiet palette sets up each punch.
steps: Keep the river of quarters steady and soft as your baseline.
In each bar, pick one beat to accent — vary which beat from bar to bar.
Reserve your most striking allowed note (the highest, or the most 'outside' sounding) for the accent.
Let the surrounding quiet quarters be repetitive — that repetition makes the accent land harder.
After the accent, immediately pull back to the whisper level without fading.
examples: quiet: lowest allowed → second allowed → [LOUD highest allowed] → second allowed, looped with the accent moving to beat 2, then 3, then 4
whispered cycle of the two lowest allowed notes with a sudden punchy top-of-palette accent once per bar
forbidden_river_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting the same allowed note every bar. When the palette shrinks it's tempting to always spotlight the same note — force yourself to rotate the accent through at least two different allowed notes.

forbidden_river_forte.interaction: Loud steady quarters with a limited palette is confident mastery practice — no dynamic variety to distract, no rhythmic tricks, just you stating your restricted vocabulary with full chest. It reveals whether you actually trust the allowed notes or are still grasping for the forbidden ones.
steps: Commit to full volume from the first note — no ramp up.
Lock the quarter pulse and don't let it bend even when your hand wants to reach for a forbidden note.
Sing or play each allowed note like you mean it — no apologetic notes in forte.
Rotate through all allowed palette notes within the first few bars so the ear hears the full available set.
When you feel the urge to 'escape' the palette, double down and repeat a single allowed note boldly instead.
examples: all allowed notes ascending in quarters, then descending, repeated at full volume like a statement
a three-note cycle through the middle of the palette repeated loud and steady, then expanding to include the top and bottom allowed notes
forbidden_river_forte.watchOut: Reaching for a forbidden note out of habit when the loudness makes you feel exposed. Forte amplifies every mistake — if you leak a forbidden note, it will be the loudest sound in the room.

forbidden_burst_swell.interaction: Fast flurries from a shrunk palette, shaped by a swelling volume arc — the bursts become gestures that grow and recede. You'll have 4-6 notes per burst to work with a small vocabulary, so repetition within a burst is not only allowed, it's essential.
steps: Plan each burst around 2-3 allowed notes — don't try to use every palette note in a single burst.
Pack the notes tight at the start of each phrase, then go silent for 2-3 beats.
Shape the volume across the whole round: early bursts barely audible, middle bursts full, final bursts fading.
Use the silence between bursts to re-choose which allowed notes to feature next.
Let each burst's loudest note fall on a different allowed palette member as the swell peaks.
examples: burst 1 (quiet): second allowed → third allowed → second allowed → first allowed, then rest; burst 2 louder on a different subset of the palette
a six-note flurry ping-ponging between the two highest allowed notes, growing louder each repetition, then silence
forbidden_burst_swell.watchOut: Speed collapsing your accuracy — when you burst fast, fingers or vocal muscles default to familiar patterns that may include forbidden notes. Slow the burst if you feel habit taking over.

forbidden_burst_terraces.interaction: Flurries from a limited palette, each burst parked on its own hard volume level — the restriction makes every terrace a declaration. You're saying the same few notes at totally different volumes, and the contrast reframes them each time.
steps: Decide three volume terraces before you start (e.g., pianissimo, mezzo, forte).
Assign each burst to one terrace — don't shift volume mid-burst.
Use a tight 2-3 note subset of the allowed palette per burst, then rest.
Rotate which terrace you visit so the listener hears at least three distinct volume rooms across the round.
Keep the rest between bursts clean — no rustling, no anticipation.
examples: quiet burst: lowest two allowed notes stuttered, rest; loud burst: highest two allowed notes stuttered, rest; medium burst: middle allowed note repeated, rest
four loud quick notes on a single allowed pitch, rest, four whispered notes cycling three allowed pitches, rest
forbidden_burst_terraces.watchOut: Bleeding the terrace volume across bursts. Each burst is its own contained statement. If burst 3 sounds like a halfway point between burst 2 and burst 4, you're fading instead of stepping.

forbidden_burst_whisper.interaction: Tight flurries from a limited palette, all at whisper volume — intense concentration in a small space. The bursts still have to feel packed and urgent, but the volume strips away any bluster and exposes every allowed-note choice.
steps: Commit to the softest volume you can cleanly articulate.
Keep bursts short — 4 notes is plenty when whispering.
Use the close spacing of the bursts to explore tiny pitch distinctions within the allowed palette.
Let the rest periods be long and honest; whispered silence is powerful.
If a burst comes out louder than intended, rest an extra beat and restart quieter.
examples: a whispered four-note burst cycling three of the lowest allowed notes, two beats rest, repeat with a slightly different three-note subset
five quick whispered notes all on the same middle allowed pitch, then silence, then five on an adjacent allowed pitch
forbidden_burst_whisper.watchOut: Crescendoing inside the burst because your hand or voice gets excited by the speed. Whisper means whisper start to whisper end, every burst.

forbidden_burst_accentMap.interaction: Flurries from a reduced palette with a single loud punch per bar — the bursts are mostly quiet, and one note inside each burst gets the spotlight. The limited palette means you keep choosing which allowed note earns the accent.
steps: Set the base burst volume to soft — 4-6 quick quiet notes with rest after.
Inside each burst, accent exactly one note much louder than the others.
Move the accent position within the burst — sometimes first note, sometimes middle, sometimes last.
Rotate the accent through different allowed notes across bars so the ear hears the palette's range.
Let the rest after each burst reset you completely before the next one.
examples: quiet: second allowed → [LOUD first allowed] → third allowed → second allowed, rest; next burst the accent moves to the third note of the burst
four whispered notes on the lowest allowed, then a sudden loud strike on the highest allowed, then silence
forbidden_burst_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting a forbidden note by mistake because the loud punch pulls your hand toward familiar territory. The accent must come from inside the palette, never from outside it.

forbidden_burst_forte.interaction: Loud fast flurries from a shrunk palette — aggressive, confident, and limited. This combo teaches you that mastery isn't about having lots of notes; it's about owning the few you have so completely that volume and density become the message.
steps: Commit to full volume on every note in every burst.
Pack each burst tight and resolve into rest — the contrast between dense loud bursts and clean silence is the whole shape.
Rotate your allowed-note subset per burst so listeners hear the full palette across the round.
Don't play louder to compensate for the missing notes — the confidence comes from how you place them, not volume alone.
Use the rest between bursts to mentally pre-hear the next burst's palette choice.
examples: six loud fast notes rolling through the top three allowed pitches, rest, then six through the bottom three allowed pitches
a loud five-note burst hammering a single middle allowed pitch, rest, then a loud flurry cycling the upper allowed pitches
forbidden_burst_forte.watchOut: Letting speed drag you into forbidden notes. At full volume and speed, muscle memory takes over and a leaked forbidden note will be the loudest thing in the room. Slow down if it happens twice.

forbidden_space_swell.interaction: Mostly silence, a handful of allowed notes, and a swelling volume arc — this is the zen combo. With so much space and such a limited palette, every single note becomes a sculpture. The swell ties the sparse notes together across the whole round.
steps: Reduce to one or two notes per bar at most.
Start each note from true silence and let it breathe before the next.
Let the swell unfold across multiple bars, not within a single note — this is a long shape.
Use the silence to consciously choose the next allowed note; don't play on autopilot.
End the round on the quietest single allowed note, held as long as feels right.
examples: one quiet allowed note in bar 1, rest most of bar 2, a medium-volume allowed note in bar 3, rest, forte allowed note in bar 4, rest, back to quiet
a single held note from the palette per bar, each note a different allowed member, the whole sequence swelling across four bars
forbidden_space_swell.watchOut: Filling the silence because it feels uncomfortable. The whole point of this combo is that the rests are doing as much work as the notes. If you hear yourself adding notes to kill silence, pause and breathe.
forbidden_space_swell.deeperInsight: Forbidden+space is where real ear development happens. You're forced to decide: of my three or four allowed notes, which one deserves this rare moment? That decision muscle is what separates improvisers from pattern players.

forbidden_space_terraces.interaction: Mostly silence punctuated by allowed notes at sharply distinct volume levels — the limited palette means the same few notes get recast as different statements by the terraced dynamics. Sparse, deliberate, architectural.
steps: Pick three clear volume terraces before you start.
Play one or two allowed notes per bar, each on its chosen terrace.
Switch terraces cleanly — no fading between loud and soft.
Repeat the same allowed note on different terraces to hear how volume reframes identity.
Keep the silence honest; rushing between notes kills the terraced contrast.
examples: quiet lowest allowed note, long rest, loud highest allowed note, long rest, medium middle allowed note, long rest
the same single middle-palette note played three times with long rests between: first quiet, then loud, then medium
forbidden_space_terraces.watchOut: Letting the second note in a pair ramp up from the first. Each note is a standalone terrace. If there's any connection in volume between two notes, you're fading.

forbidden_space_whisper.interaction: The emptiest, quietest corner of the whole system — a tiny palette, mostly silence, barely audible. This is deep listening practice. You're not really playing notes anymore; you're placing them.
steps: Reduce to one allowed note per bar, sometimes none.
Commit to the softest volume you can cleanly produce.
Let silences be unmarked — no tension, just space.
When you choose a note, commit fully to which allowed palette member it is; never half-play.
End with a long rest rather than a final note.
examples: a whispered lowest allowed note in bar 1, full silence in bar 2, whispered second allowed note in bar 3, full silence in bar 4
two whispered allowed notes spaced two bars apart, both from the bottom of the palette
forbidden_space_whisper.watchOut: Playing too many notes. The instinct is to fill, but with a small palette and whisper volume, the silence is where the music lives. If you play more than one note per bar, you're probably over-filling.

forbidden_space_accentMap.interaction: Mostly silence and a tiny palette, with one punchy loud note per bar — the sparse background makes that single accent enormous. With limited note choices, the decision of which allowed note gets the accent becomes the whole round's melody.
steps: Set the baseline: mostly silence, occasional whispered allowed notes.
In each bar, place exactly one loud accent on a carefully chosen allowed note.
Rotate which allowed note gets the accent from bar to bar — never twice in a row.
Keep everything around the accent soft and sparse so the punch lands hard.
Let the silence after the accent stretch before you softly continue.
examples: silence, then a loud highest allowed note on beat 3, silence into next bar, then a loud lowest allowed note on beat 2
a single whispered allowed note on beat 1, then a loud different allowed note on beat 4, long rest
forbidden_space_accentMap.watchOut: Letting the accent drift toward the same favorite allowed note every bar. The palette is already small — if you only accent one of them, you've effectively reduced it further.

forbidden_space_forte.interaction: Loud sparse notes from a shrunk palette — each note is a statement carved out of silence. No hiding, no dynamic shaping, just the few allowed notes announced with full conviction into open space.
steps: Play one or two notes per bar at full volume.
Let rest take up the majority of every bar — silence is part of the statement.
Commit fully to which allowed note you play; no tentative attacks at forte.
Rotate through all allowed palette notes within the first several bars.
Don't shorten notes to cram more in — the sparseness is the point.
examples: loud lowest allowed note, long rest, loud highest allowed note, long rest, loud middle allowed note, long rest
a loud held allowed note filling the first half of a bar, silence for the rest, repeated with different allowed notes each bar
forbidden_space_forte.watchOut: Adding notes because forte feels exposed in silence. The combo's power comes from one loud note in lots of space — don't panic-fill.

forbidden_offbeat_swell.interaction: Notes between the beats, from a limited palette, shaped by a growing-and-releasing volume arc. The offbeats already make the groove feel suspended, and the restricted notes amplify that floating quality — the swell gives it breath.
steps: Feel the beat internally without playing on 1 or 3.
Place your allowed notes on the 'and' of each beat, or on 2 and 4.
Begin the round barely audible and grow over 4-8 bars to full volume, then release back down.
Repeat allowed notes unashamedly — the rhythmic displacement keeps it interesting even with few pitch choices.
Let the peak of the swell land on your boldest allowed note.
examples: quiet on 'and' of 1, rest on 2, lowest allowed on 'and' of 2, rest on 3, second allowed on 'and' of 3 — looping with growing volume
a two-note cycle between the top two allowed pitches, all on offbeats, swelling from whisper to forte across the round
forbidden_offbeat_swell.watchOut: Drifting onto the downbeat when you swell — louder dynamics tempt your body to 'land' on 1. The whole point is that the beats stay empty. Tap your foot on the beats so your ear tracks the silence there.

forbidden_offbeat_terraces.interaction: Offbeat notes from a limited palette, switching between hard dynamic levels — the rhythmic displacement and the volume walls work together to make the few allowed notes feel constantly reframed. Angular, playful, architectural.
steps: Lock offbeat placement: never on 1 or 3.
Pick three volume terraces and assign bars or beat groups to each.
Switch terraces cleanly — no fade.
Let the same allowed note appear at multiple terraces across the round so you hear it reframed.
Keep the downbeats silent as a religious rule.
examples: quiet bar of offbeat lowest-allowed repeated, loud bar of offbeat highest-allowed repeated, medium bar of offbeat middle-allowed repeated
a four-note offbeat cycle through the palette, first pass whispered, second pass forte, third pass medium
forbidden_offbeat_terraces.watchOut: Accenting the downbeat by accident when you change terraces — your ear expects dynamic shifts on strong beats. Keep the terraces tied to the offbeats only.

forbidden_offbeat_whisper.interaction: Whispered offbeats from a tiny palette — ghostly, hovering, almost incorporeal. The groove is implied by what's missing, the notes are barely there, and the palette is pared down to essentials. Intimacy through restriction.
steps: Set your volume to the quietest sustainable.
Place every note on an offbeat — the 'and' of 1, 2, 3, or 4.
Use only 2-3 allowed notes for long stretches; variety isn't the goal here.
Let some offbeats be silent too — not every 'and' needs a note.
Feel the downbeat internally as a pulse you're dancing around, not landing on.
examples: whispered lowest allowed on 'and' of 2, rest, whispered second allowed on 'and' of 4, rest — repeated softly
a whispered three-note cycle through the palette, one note per offbeat, skipping occasional offbeats for extra space
forbidden_offbeat_whisper.watchOut: Getting louder as you focus. The concentration required to stay off the beat pulls volume up. Check every 8 bars that you're still whispering.

forbidden_offbeat_accentMap.interaction: Quiet offbeats from a limited palette with one loud punch per bar — the accent is always an offbeat too, so it never 'lands' where the listener expects. The restricted palette sharpens the accent choice: of your few allowed notes, which one gets the sudden shout?
steps: Keep all notes on offbeats; downbeats stay silent.
Make most offbeats quiet whispered allowed notes.
Once per bar, punch a single offbeat loud on a chosen allowed note.
Rotate which offbeat position gets the accent (the 'and' of 1, 2, 3, or 4) and which allowed note lands there.
Let the silence on downbeats feel present, not accidental.
examples: quiet lowest allowed on 'and' of 1, [LOUD highest allowed] on 'and' of 2, quiet second allowed on 'and' of 3, quiet middle allowed on 'and' of 4
mostly whispered offbeat cycles of two allowed notes, with one sudden loud offbeat strike on the top allowed note per bar
forbidden_offbeat_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting into the downbeat — the body wants to punch on 1 or 3 because that's where accents naturally live. Every accent must stay between the beats.

forbidden_offbeat_forte.interaction: Loud offbeats from a limited palette — reggae-adjacent, aggressive, defiant. The volume is full and the downbeats are empty, and the small palette makes every choice count. This is confident constraint.
steps: Commit to full volume on every offbeat.
Place nothing on 1 or 3 — the downbeats are a loud silence.
Use the limited palette without apology; repeat allowed notes proudly.
Let the forbidden notes stay forbidden even when loudness tempts reflexes.
Feel the empty downbeats as stronger than any note — they're the groove.
examples: loud middle allowed on 'and' of 1, silence on 2, loud middle allowed on 'and' of 2, silence on 3, loud highest allowed on 'and' of 3 — repeating
a loud offbeat two-note cycle between the lowest and highest allowed pitches, the downbeats as strong silent pillars
forbidden_offbeat_forte.watchOut: The volume pulling your attack onto the downbeat — forte rhythms naturally want to 'land.' If you feel yourself leaning into beats 1 or 3, consciously lift your hand or voice on those beats.

forbidden_rhythmSeed_swell.interaction: A tight repeating rhythmic cell with pitch varied each time, using a shrunk palette, shaped by a volume swell — the rhythm becomes a frame and the limited palette becomes the paint. The swell ties everything into one big gesture.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell (3-5 notes) and lock it.
Every repetition keeps the rhythm identical.
Vary the pitches on each repetition using only allowed notes.
Start the first repetition quiet and swell across the whole round.
Force yourself to find new pitch combinations within the palette on each repeat — no identical repetitions.
examples: cell: short-short-long. Rep 1 (quiet): lowest → second → first allowed. Rep 2 (louder): second → third → first allowed. Rep 3 (loud): highest → middle → lowest allowed.
a four-note galloping cell, each repetition reshuffling the same three allowed notes into a new order while the volume grows
forbidden_rhythmSeed_swell.watchOut: Changing the rhythm when you run out of pitch ideas. The rhythm is a commitment — if you feel stuck, repeat an allowed note rather than break the cell.

forbidden_rhythmSeed_terraces.interaction: A repeated rhythmic cell on hard-switched volume levels, filled with a limited palette — each terrace shows the same cell in a new light, and the restricted notes make the variations feel chiseled. Disciplined, architectural, hypnotic.
steps: Choose a short rhythmic cell and hold it fiercely.
Assign each repetition to a terrace — quiet, medium, loud — and switch cleanly.
Within each repetition, pick a different allowed-note combination.
Don't fade between reps — wall-jump the volume.
Let the terraces rotate unpredictably rather than climb in order.
examples: cell: three quick notes. Rep 1 quiet on lowest three allowed. Rep 2 loud on highest three allowed. Rep 3 medium on middle three allowed.
a two-note cell alternating between the lowest and highest allowed notes, each repetition on a different volume terrace
forbidden_rhythmSeed_terraces.watchOut: Letting the rhythm drift as you focus on the volume change. Set the cell, then the terrace — rhythm first, always.

forbidden_rhythmSeed_whisper.interaction: A tiny rhythmic cell repeated quietly with a restricted palette — meditative, intimate, almost mantra-like. The soft volume means the cell's rhythm is the clearest thing in the room, and the limited pitches become a small prayer cycle.
steps: Pick a short, comfortable rhythmic cell you can loop without losing.
Keep whisper volume throughout.
Vary the pitches within the palette from repetition to repetition, but only slightly — this is meditation, not exploration.
Let some repetitions use only a single allowed note.
Feel free to hold the cell loop for many bars — monotony is allowed here.
examples: cell: two quick notes + rest. Whispered reps cycle between the two lowest allowed notes, then add the third allowed note, then drop back to two.
a four-note whispered cell, each repetition recoloring the same rhythm with a slightly different allowed-note combination
forbidden_rhythmSeed_whisper.watchOut: Rushing the cell because whisper removes the volume anchor. Use your body — breath, sway, foot tap — to hold the rhythm steady.

forbidden_rhythmSeed_accentMap.interaction: A repeated rhythmic cell, mostly quiet with one loud punch per bar, filled with a restricted palette — the accent always hits inside the cell, and the limited notes make the accented moment a recurring highlight you get to sculpt.
steps: Lock a short rhythmic cell.
Play all repetitions quiet except for one note per bar.
Rotate which position within the cell gets the accent — first note, middle note, last note.
Choose the accented allowed note deliberately; don't default to the same one.
Keep the rhythm absolutely steady even when the accent falls on an unusual position.
examples: cell: four quick notes. Quiet: lowest → second → third → second allowed. Then next bar accent the second note: [LOUD] second → quiet third → second → lowest.
a three-note cell, each bar accenting a different note of the cell on a different allowed pitch, quiet everywhere else
forbidden_rhythmSeed_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting outside the cell by adding an extra note. The accent is inside the repeating rhythm — not an interruption of it.

forbidden_rhythmSeed_forte.interaction: A repeated rhythmic cell at full volume with a limited palette — mantra-like, insistent, powerful. The constant rhythm plus restricted notes plus full volume creates a trance state where every micro-variation in pitch choice feels enormous.
steps: Pick a strong, punchy rhythmic cell.
Play it at full volume every repetition.
Vary only the pitches, and only within the allowed palette.
Let reps repeat the same pitch combination twice or three times if it feels right.
Stay locked in the cell until the round ends — no drifting off the form.
examples: cell: dotted short + long + short. Loud reps cycling pitches: lowest→middle→lowest, then second→highest→second, then back
a five-note loud cell, each repetition shuffling the top three allowed pitches into a new order
forbidden_rhythmSeed_forte.watchOut: Letting volume break the cell. Forte tempts extra notes and fills. The rhythm is the commitment — if your hand wants to add flourish, redirect that energy into stronger attack on the existing cell notes.

forbidden_triplets_swell.interaction: Rolling triplets from a shrunk palette, with a volume swell across the round — the triplet flow has enough density to keep momentum even with few notes, and the swell gives the whole thing a breathing, organic arc.
steps: Lock three notes per beat — no switching to duple.
Use the triplet density to let repeated allowed notes feel natural.
Begin near silence and grow the volume across 4-8 bars, then release.
Let the triplets circle a small subset of the palette — 2-3 notes is plenty.
At the swell peak, use your fullest allowed-note range; at the quiet ends, shrink to 1-2 notes.
examples: triplets cycling lowest→second→third allowed, quiet at first, swelling to loud by bar 4, then back to quiet on the same three notes
triplet repetitions of two allowed notes only, growing loud, then expanding to three allowed notes at the peak, then contracting back to two
forbidden_triplets_swell.watchOut: The triplet feel flattening into even eighths when you focus on the swell. Count 'one-trip-let' in your head to keep the rolling three-per-beat pulse.

forbidden_triplets_terraces.interaction: Rolling triplets from a limited palette, switching between hard volume terraces — the constant triplet flow plus the abrupt dynamic walls creates a stuttering architectural feel, and the small palette keeps it disciplined.
steps: Commit to triplets every beat.
Switch terraces at bar lines or every two bars, no fade.
Assign a different allowed-note subset to each terrace if you want extra contrast.
Don't slow the triplets when switching volume — the flow is the constant.
Use at least three distinct volume levels across the round.
examples: quiet bar of triplets on the bottom two allowed notes, loud bar of triplets on the top two allowed notes, medium bar of triplets on the middle allowed notes
triplet cycle through three allowed pitches, first pass whispered, second pass forte, third pass mezzo, no fades between
forbidden_triplets_terraces.watchOut: Crescendoing into a new terrace because the triplet speed creates momentum that feels like a ramp. The switch must be a wall — triplets continue at the old volume up until the exact beat of the change.

forbidden_triplets_whisper.interaction: Rolling triplets at whisper volume from a restricted palette — cascading, liquid, almost inaudible. The triplet density combined with soft volume creates a shimmering texture, and the limited palette keeps it focused and meditative.
steps: Whisper every note — the softest sustainable volume.
Hold rolling triplets throughout — no breaks into duple.
Let the palette be tiny — 2-3 allowed notes is often enough.
Use gentle articulation; don't accent any triplet.
Breathe or relax your hand every few bars without breaking the flow.
examples: whispered triplets cycling two allowed notes only, for eight bars, then adding a third allowed note for variety
a slow-rolling triplet texture on three allowed notes, each triplet barely audible, like rain
forbidden_triplets_whisper.watchOut: The first note of each beat getting louder than the other two. Triplet feels often have a built-in accent on beat 1 of the group — consciously suppress that at whisper volume.

forbidden_triplets_accentMap.interaction: Rolling triplets at low volume from a limited palette, with one loud accent per bar — the triplet texture stays mostly quiet and the single accent stands up out of the flow like a flash. The restricted palette means each accent lands on one of a few carefully chosen allowed notes.
steps: Lock triplet flow throughout.
Keep baseline volume low — quiet but not whisper.
Choose one triplet per bar to accent loudly.
Rotate the accent position within the bar — sometimes the first triplet of beat 2, sometimes the third triplet of beat 4.
Rotate the accented allowed note from bar to bar.
examples: quiet triplet flow on the bottom three allowed notes, with one sudden loud accent on the top allowed note on a different triplet each bar
soft triplets cycling the middle of the palette, with a loud punch on the lowest allowed note once per bar, accent position moving
forbidden_triplets_accentMap.watchOut: The accent speeding up or slowing the triplet flow around it. The rhythm must stay absolutely locked — the accent is a volume event, not a rhythmic one.

forbidden_triplets_forte.interaction: Full-volume rolling triplets from a shrunk palette — dense, powerful, relentless. The triplet flow carries huge momentum, the loudness commits you fully, and the limited palette means you're mastering a small vocabulary at maximum energy. Pure conviction practice.
steps: Commit to full volume on every triplet.
Never break the rolling three-per-beat feel.
Cycle aggressively through the allowed palette.
Repeat the same allowed note across multiple triplets if you want — it will feel intentional at forte.
Use all allowed notes at least once within the first few bars, then settle into your favorite subset.
examples: loud rolling triplets ascending and descending through all allowed palette notes, over and over
a loud triplet pattern hammering two allowed notes for a bar, then expanding to three allowed notes, then contracting back
forbidden_triplets_forte.watchOut: Leaking a forbidden note under the pressure of speed and volume. Forte triplets are the most dangerous combo for muscle-memory mistakes — if you hear a forbidden note slip in, slow the triplet feel briefly until your hand resets.

targetLanding_river_swell.interaction: Even quarter notes give you a steady conveyor belt while the swell shapes a long arc — the target note should crest at the swell's peak and resolve into the silence at the end. Every phrase becomes a breath that lands home.
steps: Set a calm quarter-note pulse and let the first notes be barely audible wandering.
Grow the volume gradually as you walk through the scale, saving your biggest sound for when the target arrives.
Time the wandering so the target lands right at the peak of the swell, then fade to silence.
Between phrases, reset to barely-audible and start wandering again toward the same destination.
Let each phrase be a 4-8 bar arc with the target always as the climax.
Rest a full bar between swells to hear the gravity reset.
examples: Wander up four steps → drop down two → step up to the target at the loudest point → fade to nothing (8 quarters).
Start on the fifth below → meander across three neighbors → crest on target as the swell peaks → let the tone bloom then vanish.
Begin a whole step above target → drift up five steps → curve back down stepwise → land on target right as volume maxes out.
targetLanding_river_swell.watchOut: Rushing to the target before the swell has fully grown — the dynamic arc and the pitch arc must arrive together. Don't peak early.
targetLanding_river_swell.deeperInsight: The target is both the harmonic home and the dynamic summit. When they align, the ear feels a double resolution.

targetLanding_river_terraces.interaction: Quarter notes march steadily while volume switches in hard blocks — the target becomes the anchor that sounds true whether it arrives loud or soft. Try landing it at different terrace levels to hear how the same note changes character.
steps: Pick two or three volume levels (quiet, medium, loud) and lock them in before you start.
Wander in quarter notes at one level for a phrase, then snap to a different level for the next phrase.
Make sure every phrase still lands on the target regardless of which volume terrace you're in.
Try landing the target at a different volume each time to hear how its character shifts.
No fades — when you change volume, change it instantly between phrases.
examples: Quiet wander up three steps then down to target (4 quarters) → snap loud → wander down four steps then up to target (4 quarters).
Medium phrase curving through five neighbors → target → snap quiet → same shape reversed → target again at the new level.
Loud phrase zigzagging through the top of the scale → target → immediate drop to whisper → slow step down to target.
targetLanding_river_terraces.watchOut: Accidentally smoothing the volume change into a fade, or losing the target when you focus on the terrace shift. The snap should feel architectural, not expressive.

targetLanding_river_whisper.interaction: Whisper volume makes every note delicate, and the steady quarter flow turns each landing on the target into a tiny private event. The target becomes a secret you keep returning to.
steps: Drop to the quietest sound you can produce cleanly and hold that level the whole round.
Walk in even quarter notes through the scale, letting each note barely breathe.
Land every phrase on the target, but don't emphasize it — let the pattern of return do the work.
Keep phrases short (4-6 notes) so the target arrives frequently.
Listen for the feeling of 'coming home' each time you touch it, even at a whisper.
examples: Tiptoe up two steps → back one → down to target (4 quarters, all barely audible).
Barely-there curve through three neighbors above target → step down → land on target.
Hovering three-note figure around a middle note → drifting down to target as a soft arrival.
targetLanding_river_whisper.watchOut: Pushing volume when you feel the target approaching. The temptation is to announce home — resist it. The target should sound the same as everything else.
targetLanding_river_whisper.deeperInsight: At whisper volume, the listener leans in. The target's gravity is felt rather than heard.

targetLanding_river_accentMap.interaction: The target note IS the accent in this combo — quiet quarter-note wandering broken once per bar by a punchy arrival at home. This makes the pull of the target impossible to ignore.
steps: Walk quietly in quarter notes, wandering freely through the scale.
Time your phrases so exactly one punchy loud note lands per bar — and make that note the target whenever possible.
The rest of the bar stays quiet, no matter how interesting the wandering gets.
Let the ear learn: accent = home.
Vary the approach to the target so the accent always feels fresh even though the pitch repeats.
examples: Quiet walk up three steps → loud punch on target (beat 4) → quiet step-down wander → loud punch on target next bar.
Quiet meander through five neighbors above target → target as accent → quiet curve down → target as accent again.
Soft ascent through most of the scale → sudden target punch from above → soft descent → target punch from below.
targetLanding_river_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting notes that aren't the target, which dilutes the effect. The discipline is: one accent per bar, and it's home.
targetLanding_river_accentMap.deeperInsight: This combo is teaching your body that arrival and emphasis belong together — a core principle of phrasing.

targetLanding_river_forte.interaction: Full volume quarter notes turn the target into a drumbeat — every phrase lands like a fist on the table. The steady pulse and unrelenting volume make the gravity of home feel physical.
steps: Play strong, steady quarter notes at full volume throughout.
Wander confidently through the scale, never dropping volume, never hesitating.
Let every phrase end firmly on the target, with the same weight as the other notes.
Reset with a tiny breath between phrases but don't drop volume — just pause.
Feel the target arriving again and again like a heartbeat at the end of each thought.
examples: Strong walk up five steps → turn and march back down to target (8 quarters, all forte).
Bold zigzag through three high neighbors → swoop down four steps → land on target.
Confident step-up sequence from two below target → up past it → back down → land on target.
targetLanding_river_forte.watchOut: Bashing without shape. Even at full volume, the wandering needs direction and the target needs clear arrival — it's not a wall of noise, it's an anthem.

targetLanding_burst_swell.interaction: Bursts pack 4-6 quick notes into the front of each phrase, and in this combo the target must be the last note of every burst — the place the burst flies toward. The swell shapes the burst itself, starting quiet and cresting right as the target hits.
steps: Plan each burst as a little melodic flight that ends on the target.
Start each burst barely audible and grow to full volume right as the target lands.
After the target, drop to silence for 2-3 beats before the next burst.
Keep the bursts fast — 4-6 notes over about a beat.
Vary the burst shape but never the destination.
examples: Quiet burst: fifth above target → fourth above → third above → target (loud) → silence for 3 beats.
Whispered burst: neighbor above → neighbor below → neighbor above → target (crest) → rest.
Soft burst zigzagging down from four steps above → landing loudly on target → long silence.
targetLanding_burst_swell.watchOut: Starting the burst already loud, which flattens the swell, or letting the burst end on a non-target note. Every burst must crest on home.
targetLanding_burst_swell.deeperInsight: Burst + swell + target landing = the phrase IS the arrival. There's no separation between shape, dynamic, and destination.

targetLanding_burst_terraces.interaction: Bursts fly fast and land on the target; terraces make each burst happen at a different volume level with no fading. You're choosing which terrace this burst lives on and letting the target anchor it.
steps: Pick your volume levels — two or three — before starting.
Rip a burst at one level, landing on the target, then rest.
For the next burst, snap to a different level before the first note and stay there through the whole burst.
The target lands at whatever level that burst is living in.
Keep bursts tight (4-6 notes) with 2-3 beats of silence between.
examples: Quiet burst wandering up three steps then down to target → rest → loud burst flying down four steps to target → rest.
Medium burst curving around neighbors → target → snap to quiet burst with the same shape → target again.
Loud burst from five above target → target → rest → whisper burst from two below target → target.
targetLanding_burst_terraces.watchOut: Letting the burst's energy drag the volume up or down. Each burst must stay fully inside its chosen terrace.

targetLanding_burst_whisper.interaction: Bursts at a whisper are quick, delicate flurries — like a hummingbird brushing the target. The silence between bursts becomes huge, and each landing on home feels intimate.
steps: Drop to whisper volume and commit to staying there.
Play tight bursts of 4-6 quick notes, all delicate, all ending on the target.
Rest 2-3 beats between bursts to let the silence frame the landing.
Don't let intensity sneak into your volume — keep it featherweight even when the fingers or voice move fast.
Land each burst on the target with the same delicate weight as every other note in the burst.
examples: Feathery burst up three steps then down to target → long silence → another soft burst with a new contour → target.
Whispered flurry zigzagging around the target → brushing down to land on it → rest.
Tiny burst from two above target → above → target → silence → tiny burst from two below → target.
targetLanding_burst_whisper.watchOut: Speed triggering volume — when you play fast your body wants to push. Keep the whisper even through the rush.
targetLanding_burst_whisper.deeperInsight: Fast and quiet is harder than fast and loud. This combo builds dynamic independence.

targetLanding_burst_accentMap.interaction: Bursts naturally end on the target, and the target IS the accent — so the accent map becomes automatic: quiet flurry, loud target, silence. The loud note at the end of each burst is home, like a period at the end of a sentence.
steps: Play each burst quiet — 4-6 fast notes at low volume.
Hit the target at the END of the burst as the one loud note of the bar.
Rest 2-3 beats before the next burst.
The accent and the landing are the same event.
Vary the burst shape freely, but never the placement or volume of the target.
examples: Quiet burst up three steps → loud target → rest → quiet burst down four steps → loud target → rest.
Soft flurry curving around neighbors → punchy target to end it → silence → soft flurry zigzagging → punchy target.
Whispered burst from five above target → target (sudden forte) → long rest → repeat with a different approach.
targetLanding_burst_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting a non-target note inside the burst, which confuses the map. Only the target gets the punch.
targetLanding_burst_accentMap.deeperInsight: Accent maps to destination — a powerful lesson in how dynamics and harmony collaborate.

targetLanding_burst_forte.interaction: Full-volume bursts are explosive arrivals — the target lands like a cannon at the end of each flurry. This is the most physical version of target landing: every phrase is an aimed missile hitting home.
steps: Play each burst loud — 4-6 fast notes at full volume.
Aim every burst so the last note is the target, landing with full weight.
Rest 2-3 beats after each burst — the silence matters as much as the burst.
Vary the approach path but keep the target as the destination.
Feel the forward drive of each burst pulling toward home.
examples: Loud burst up from a fifth below → stepping up through the scale → landing on target → silence.
Fast descent from four above target → sidestep through neighbors → slam target → rest.
Burst zigzagging around the midrange → sweeping up → landing forte on target → rest.
targetLanding_burst_forte.watchOut: Losing accuracy in the rush. A loud burst that misses the target sounds chaotic — commit to the landing even when the fingers are flying.

targetLanding_space_swell.interaction: Space leaves most of each bar empty; the swell gives you a long slow arc. The target becomes the resolution in a sea of silence — a note that finally lands after a long grow-and-fade wander.
steps: Play one or two notes per bar, barely audible at first.
Let the phrase grow over 4-8 bars, with each sparse note a little louder than the last.
Time the wandering so the target lands at the peak of the swell, then fades to silence through the following bars.
Use the rests to feel the tension of not-yet-home.
After the target arrives and fades, begin a new quiet phrase.
examples: Quiet note two steps above target (bar 1) → silence → softer neighbor (bar 2) → building → target at the peak (bar 4) → fade to silence over bars 5-6.
Barely audible fifth above target → rest → neighbor → rest → step down → swell climbing → target → tail of silence.
Soft high note → rest → drifting down by thirds → peak volume → target as arrival → long fade.
targetLanding_space_swell.watchOut: Filling the space with nervous extra notes. Trust the rests — they're not empty, they're loaded.
targetLanding_space_swell.deeperInsight: When silence is most of the music, the target carries the weight of the whole phrase. Every rest is anticipation.

targetLanding_space_terraces.interaction: Spacious notes at distinct volume terraces — each sparse note is a statement, and each phrase's target arrives at whichever level the phrase is living in. The silence exposes every volume choice.
steps: Play one or two notes per bar, half or more of the bar in silence.
Pick a volume terrace for each phrase (quiet, medium, loud) and hold it.
Wander sparsely through the scale, always ending the phrase on the target at the chosen level.
Snap to a new terrace for the next phrase — no fade.
Let the silence make each terrace shift feel architectural.
examples: Medium note three above target → rest → step down → rest → target (all at medium) → snap loud → new phrase same shape, landing on target.
Quiet note from five above → long rest → another note closer → rest → target → snap to loud → sparse wander → target.
Loud single note → rest → loud neighbor → rest → loud target → silence → drop to quiet → sparse phrase ending on target.
targetLanding_space_terraces.watchOut: Letting silence soften your volume between notes of the same phrase. The terrace must hold even through the rests.

targetLanding_space_whisper.interaction: Whispered notes with lots of silence between them make the target feel like a prayer — each landing barely disturbs the air. This combo demands presence: every sparse, quiet note matters, and home is the quietest gift.
steps: Play only one or two notes per bar, all at whisper volume.
Let the silences stretch and breathe.
Land each phrase on the target with the same delicate weight as every other note.
Don't rush to the next phrase — let the target hang and fade naturally.
Listen hard to each note before you play the next one.
examples: Whispered note three above target → long rest → whispered neighbor → rest → target (same whisper) → long silence.
Soft single note from far above → rest → step down → rest → another step → rest → target → silence.
Barely audible fifth above → long pause → neighbor → pause → target → pause → next phrase.
targetLanding_space_whisper.watchOut: Filling the silences with fidgeting or losing the whisper when you get to the target. Home is not announced, it's offered.
targetLanding_space_whisper.deeperInsight: This combo is meditation — the target is a place you return to in stillness.

targetLanding_space_accentMap.interaction: In a bar mostly of rest, one punchy note per bar — and it's the target. The accent, the sparse texture, and the landing all converge on the same moment. This is target landing at its most stripped-down.
steps: Play one or two quiet sparse notes per bar, wandering through the scale.
In each bar, one note punches loud — and it's the target (or as close to the target as you can place it).
The rest of the bar is quiet notes and silence.
The quiet notes set up the accent; the accent is always home.
Let the silence around the accent make it feel huge.
examples: Quiet sparse wander on beats 1 and 2 → loud target on beat 3 → silence on beat 4.
Silence → quiet neighbor above on beat 2 → silence → loud target on beat 4.
Quiet note two above target on beat 1 → silence → silence → loud target on beat 4.
targetLanding_space_accentMap.watchOut: Putting the accent on the wrong note when the wandering pulls you elsewhere. Discipline the accent to the target.
targetLanding_space_accentMap.deeperInsight: Accent + target + space = the clearest possible statement of home. Great for hearing gravity.

targetLanding_space_forte.interaction: Forte with space is declarative — sparse loud notes, each one a statement, with the target as the final anchor of each phrase. The silence between notes makes every arrival feel monumental.
steps: Play one or two forte notes per bar, wandering freely through the scale.
Let the rests breathe — half or more of each bar is silent.
Land every phrase on the target with the same full volume as the other notes.
Don't rush — use the rests to point toward the target.
Treat each note like a gong strike.
examples: Loud note from five above target → rest → step down → rest → target → long rest.
Forte neighbor above target → silence → neighbor below → silence → target → silence.
Bold note from a third above → long rest → target → silence → bold fifth above → long rest → target.
targetLanding_space_forte.watchOut: Filling the silence with nervous loud notes. Trust the rests — the forte sparseness is the point.

targetLanding_offbeat_swell.interaction: Offbeat notes live between the beats — never on 1 or 3 — which creates a sideways pull. The swell then shapes the phrase so the target arrives at the loudest point, but still on an offbeat. This makes home feel both destined and surprising.
steps: Practice placing notes on the 'and' of each beat, never on the downbeat.
Start each phrase quiet and grow gradually.
Time the wandering so the target lands on an offbeat at the peak of the swell.
Fade out after the target, maintaining offbeat placement.
Let the strangeness of an offbeat target build tension the downbeat would dissipate.
examples: Quiet offbeat notes climbing through the scale → target on an 'and' at the swell's peak → fade to silence on offbeats.
Soft 'ands' zigzagging around the midrange → crest → target on an offbeat (loud) → fade.
Barely audible offbeat pattern descending from four above → target on an 'and' as swell peaks → fade.
targetLanding_offbeat_swell.watchOut: Letting the target slip onto the downbeat. The offbeat rule applies to the target too — that's what gives this combo its flavor.
targetLanding_offbeat_swell.deeperInsight: Offbeat target + swell peak = resolution without rest. The ear expects home on the downbeat, and you give it home early.

targetLanding_offbeat_terraces.interaction: Offbeat notes at distinct volume terraces — no note ever lands on 1 or 3, and volume snaps between levels. The target arrives on an offbeat at whatever terrace the current phrase lives in.
steps: Lock in 2-3 volume levels before you start.
Play phrases entirely on offbeats — 'and' of 1, 'and' of 2, etc.
Each phrase lives at one terrace; snap to a new terrace for the next phrase.
Land each phrase on the target, on an offbeat, at the current volume.
Don't let the offbeat throw off the terrace discipline — both constraints hold together.
examples: Medium offbeat wander up three steps → target on an 'and' → snap loud → offbeat descent → target on an 'and'.
Quiet offbeats zigzagging around neighbors → target on an 'and' → snap medium → new offbeat shape → target.
Loud offbeat climb → target on an 'and' → snap to quiet → offbeat descent → target on an 'and'.
targetLanding_offbeat_terraces.watchOut: Slipping onto the downbeat when you concentrate on volume snaps. Metronome helps — only the 'ands' should sound.

targetLanding_offbeat_whisper.interaction: Whispered offbeats create a secret pulse — you feel the beats in silence, and you whisper between them. The target lands on an 'and' at whisper volume, a tiny answer that arrives sideways.
steps: Drop to whisper volume and lock in an offbeat feel.
Play sparse soft notes on the 'ands,' letting the downbeats stay silent.
Wander through the scale in offbeats, landing each phrase on the target.
Don't emphasize the target — let it arrive as softly as any other note.
Keep phrases short so the target recurs and the pull stays alive.
examples: Whispered offbeat from three above target → offbeat neighbor → offbeat step down → whispered offbeat target.
Tiny 'ands' zigzagging around the midrange → whispered target on an 'and' → pause → repeat with a new contour.
Barely audible offbeats descending from four above → target on an 'and' → long silence → another phrase.
targetLanding_offbeat_whisper.watchOut: Volume creeping up when you focus on the offbeat placement. Both constraints — whisper AND offbeat — must hold simultaneously.

targetLanding_offbeat_accentMap.interaction: One loud note per bar, and it's the target — but it lands on an offbeat. The accent surprises by arriving early (or late), and it's always home. This is phrasing at its most off-kilter.
steps: Play quiet offbeat wandering through the scale.
One accent per bar, on the 'and' of a beat, and it's the target.
The rest of the bar stays quiet offbeats.
The surprise accent on the offbeat makes home feel fresh every time.
Vary which 'and' the target lands on — 'and of 1,' 'and of 2,' etc.
examples: Quiet offbeat climb → loud target on 'and of 3' → quiet offbeat descent → loud target on 'and of 4' next bar.
Soft 'ands' around the midrange → punchy target on 'and of 2' → soft 'ands' continue → punchy target on 'and of 4'.
Whispered offbeat wander → loud target on 'and of 1' → quiet offbeats → loud target on 'and of 3' next bar.
targetLanding_offbeat_accentMap.watchOut: Putting the accent on a downbeat, which would break the offbeat rule. The accent must be an 'and' AND the target.
targetLanding_offbeat_accentMap.deeperInsight: This is the most syncopated version of target landing — home keeps arriving when you don't expect it.

targetLanding_offbeat_forte.interaction: Full-volume offbeats create an aggressive sideways groove — every note between the beats, every note loud, and the target always arriving on an 'and.' It's relentless and propulsive.
steps: Play full volume throughout.
Every note lands on an 'and,' never on a downbeat.
Wander boldly through the scale and land each phrase on the target, on an offbeat.
Don't drop volume between phrases — just pause briefly.
Let the off-kilter target landings drive forward.
examples: Loud offbeat climb from two below target → target on 'and of 4' → brief rest → descent → target on 'and of 4'.
Bold 'ands' zigzagging around neighbors → target on 'and of 2' → bold 'ands' reversing direction → target on 'and of 3'.
Forte offbeat descent from four above → target on 'and of 1' → bold climb → target on 'and of 4'.
targetLanding_offbeat_forte.watchOut: The downbeat wanting to sneak in when you play loud and fast. Tap your foot on the downbeat so your playing stays between.

targetLanding_rhythmSeed_swell.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats while pitches change, and the swell grows across many repetitions. The target arrives at the peak of the swell, always in the same rhythmic slot within the cell — so the ear learns to expect home at that spot.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell (e.g., two eighths and a quarter) and lock it in.
Start quiet and let the swell build over several repetitions.
In each repetition, wander through the scale but decide which slot of the cell the target will land in.
Time the peak of the swell so the target arrives at full volume in its designated slot.
Fade the cell out after the target arrives.
examples: Cell: two eighths + quarter. Start quiet → cells stepping up the scale → target in the final quarter at the swell's peak → fade.
Cell: dotted-eighth + sixteenth + quarter. Quiet cells zigzagging → target as the final quarter at peak volume → fade.
Cell: quarter + two eighths. Soft cells drifting down → target as the first quarter at the swell's peak → fade.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_swell.watchOut: Changing the cell mid-phrase, which dilutes the repetition. The rhythm is the constant — pitch and volume change, the cell doesn't.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_swell.deeperInsight: Target + rhythm seed creates two kinds of return: the cell returns rhythmically, and the target returns melodically. Layered homecoming.

targetLanding_rhythmSeed_terraces.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats at different volume terraces. The target lands in the same slot of the cell every time, but the volume changes terrace to terrace. This makes home sound different without moving.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell and commit to it.
Play a phrase of several cell repetitions at one terrace, landing the final cell on the target.
Snap to a new terrace (no fade) and play another phrase of the same cell, landing on the target.
Keep the cell identical across terraces — only pitches and volume change.
Let the target's character shift with each terrace.
examples: Cell: two eighths + quarter. Medium phrase: cells climbing → target in the final quarter → snap loud → same cell descending → target.
Cell: quarter + two eighths. Quiet cells zigzagging → target → snap medium → cells reversing → target.
Cell: dotted-eighth + sixteenth + quarter. Loud cells wandering high → target → snap quiet → cells wandering low → target.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_terraces.watchOut: Changing the cell when you change the terrace. The cell is the anchor — hold it steady while everything else moves.

targetLanding_rhythmSeed_whisper.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats at whisper volume — the cell becomes a quiet mantra, and each repetition lands on the target as a soft homecoming. Delicate and hypnotic.
steps: Drop to whisper and stay there.
Pick a short rhythmic cell and repeat it quietly throughout.
In each repetition, wander pitches while the rhythm stays identical.
Land the final slot of the cell on the target.
Let the whispered repetition feel meditative.
examples: Cell: two eighths + quarter. Whispered cells climbing → soft target in the final quarter → cells descending → soft target.
Cell: quarter + two eighths. Whispered cells zigzagging around the midrange → soft target → cells reversing → soft target.
Cell: dotted pattern. Barely audible cells drifting through neighbors → soft target → cells curving the other way → soft target.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_whisper.watchOut: Losing the cell when you focus on whispering, or pushing volume on the target. Both constraints must stay locked.

targetLanding_rhythmSeed_accentMap.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats with one accent per bar — and the accent is the target. The repeated cell gives the ear a pattern; the accent on the target makes home the punctuation of every bar.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell and repeat it throughout at a quiet base volume.
Once per bar, one note is loud — the target.
The rest of the bar is the cell at quiet volume.
Vary which slot in the cell the target accents, or keep it consistent for extra drive.
Let the cell's repetition make the accented target feel inevitable.
examples: Cell: two eighths + quarter. Quiet cells wandering → loud target as the final quarter → quiet cell continues → loud target next bar.
Cell: quarter + two eighths. Soft cells climbing → loud target as the first quarter of the new bar → soft cells continue.
Cell: dotted-eighth + sixteenth + quarter. Whispered cells zigzagging → loud target as the final quarter → whispered cells continue.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting a slot that isn't the target. The accent must match the landing, bar after bar.

targetLanding_rhythmSeed_forte.interaction: A rhythmic cell repeats at full volume, pounding home the pattern and the target in equal measure. This is the most assertive version — relentless cell, relentless volume, relentless return.
steps: Pick a short rhythmic cell and play it loud throughout.
Repeat the cell with different pitches, wandering through the scale.
Land the final slot of each cell on the target.
Don't let volume flag between cells.
Let the target arrive as one more strong note in a chain of strong notes.
examples: Cell: two eighths + quarter. Loud cells climbing from three below → target in the final quarter → cells descending → target.
Cell: quarter + two eighths. Bold cells zigzagging → target as first quarter of new cell → cells curving → target.
Cell: dotted pattern. Forte cells wandering high → target as final quarter → cells descending → target.
targetLanding_rhythmSeed_forte.watchOut: The cell becoming sloppy under volume pressure. Keep the rhythm tight even when the hand or voice wants to bash.

targetLanding_triplets_swell.interaction: Triplets roll three notes per beat and the swell grows a long arc — the target lands at the peak of the swell, on a strong triplet accent. Triplets give an even, flowing base; the target becomes the crest.
steps: Start with quiet, even triplets.
Wander pitches within the scale while keeping the three-per-beat feel.
Grow gradually across several bars.
Time the phrase so the target lands on a downbeat triplet at the swell's peak.
Fade back to quiet triplets after the target.
examples: Quiet triplets climbing from three below target → building → target on the first triplet of a beat at peak volume → fade.
Soft triplets zigzagging around neighbors → crescendo → target on a strong triplet at peak → fade.
Barely audible triplets descending from four above → building → target on a downbeat triplet at peak → fade.
targetLanding_triplets_swell.watchOut: Triplets becoming uneven when you concentrate on the swell. Use the metronome to stay locked in three-per-beat.
targetLanding_triplets_swell.deeperInsight: Triplets + swell = rolling tidal motion. The target is where the wave crests before receding.

targetLanding_triplets_terraces.interaction: Triplets at distinct volume terraces — three notes per beat, and volume snaps hard between phrases. The target lands at whichever level the current phrase lives in, as a strong triplet.
steps: Lock in 2-3 volume terraces before starting.
Play a phrase of triplets at one terrace, landing on the target.
Snap to a new terrace (no fade) and play another phrase of triplets, landing on the target.
Keep the triplet pulse even and consistent across terraces.
Let the target's character change with each terrace.
examples: Medium triplets climbing → target on a downbeat triplet → snap loud → triplets descending → target.
Quiet triplets zigzagging around neighbors → target → snap medium → new triplet contour → target.
Loud triplets wandering high → target → snap quiet → triplets wandering low → target.
targetLanding_triplets_terraces.watchOut: Triplets becoming duplets when volume changes distract you. The rhythmic feel must hold through every terrace shift.

targetLanding_triplets_whisper.interaction: Whispered triplets create a fluttery, gentle motion — three notes per beat at the softest volume, wandering toward the target each phrase. Delicate, intricate, and intimate.
steps: Drop to whisper volume.
Play even triplets — three notes per beat — at the quietest volume you can manage cleanly.
Wander through the scale, keeping the triplet feel consistent.
Land each phrase on the target as a soft triplet note.
Let the whispered flutter carry you gently home.
examples: Whispered triplets climbing from two below target → soft target on a downbeat triplet → triplets continue softly.
Barely audible triplets zigzagging around the midrange → soft target as a downbeat triplet → whispered triplets continue.
Tiny triplets drifting down from four above → soft target → triplets reverse direction → soft target again.
targetLanding_triplets_whisper.watchOut: Volume creeping up as the triplets get busy. The flutter must stay quiet even when the fingers or voice are moving fast.

targetLanding_triplets_accentMap.interaction: Triplets roll quietly and one strong note per bar is the target — the accent punches out of the triplet flow, always on the target. The three-per-beat motion makes the accented target feel like it arrives on a wave.
steps: Play quiet even triplets throughout.
Once per bar, one triplet note lands loud — and it's the target.
The rest of the bar is quiet triplets.
Let the accent mark home, bar after bar.
Vary which beat the target-accent lands on for variety.
examples: Quiet triplets climbing → loud target on beat 3 (a downbeat triplet) → quiet triplets continue → loud target next bar on beat 2.
Soft triplets zigzagging → loud target on beat 4 → soft triplets descending → loud target on beat 1 next bar.
Whispered triplets wandering → loud target on beat 2 → whispered triplets continue → loud target on beat 4.
targetLanding_triplets_accentMap.watchOut: Accenting a non-target triplet because it feels rhythmically strong. The accent must match the target, not just the beat.

targetLanding_triplets_forte.interaction: Full-volume triplets create rolling, relentless motion, and the target arrives as a strong triplet note in the flow — not louder than the others, just landing firmly as the end of each phrase. It's a tidal wave that keeps hitting home.
steps: Play full volume even triplets throughout.
Wander boldly through the scale in three-per-beat motion.
Land each phrase on the target as a strong triplet note.
Don't drop volume between phrases — just pause briefly.
Let the triplet flow and the target landings feel inevitable.
examples: Forte triplets climbing from three below target → target on a downbeat triplet → triplets descending → target.
Bold triplets zigzagging around neighbors → target on a downbeat triplet → triplets reversing → target.
Loud triplets wandering from five above target → target as a downbeat triplet → triplets curving back → target.
targetLanding_triplets_forte.watchOut: Triplets collapsing into duplets under volume pressure. Practice slowly first to make sure the three-per-beat feel holds at full volume.

questionAnswer_river_swell.interaction: Even quarter notes strip away rhythmic drama, so the swell and the conversational arc have to carry the whole shape. Your question fades in as it rises, the answer crests at the turnaround and melts back to silence on the home note.
steps: Set the drone and tap quarters for four bars before singing anything.
Shape each question as 4-6 rising quarters, starting barely audible and growing.
Let the answer begin at full voice on its highest note, then descend back to the root while fading.
Keep the quarter-note pulse perfectly even even while the volume is moving.
Breathe fully in the silence between pairs so the next question can start from true quiet.
examples: Q: root → second → third → fifth (rising, unresolved, fading in). A: fifth → fourth → second → root (descending, crests then fades home).
Q: third → fourth → fifth → sixth (rising, swelling to full). A: sixth → fifth → third → root (full to whisper, settles on home).
Q: root → third → fifth → sixth (rising line swelling in). A: fifth → third → second → root (peak, then fade to home).
questionAnswer_river_swell.watchOut: The common trap is letting the swell plateau at full volume so both phrases sound the same — make sure the question is genuinely quieter than the answer's peak, and the answer genuinely ends in silence.
questionAnswer_river_swell.deeperInsight: When rhythm is invisible, dynamics become the punctuation. Treat the volume curve as your question mark and period.

questionAnswer_river_terraces.interaction: Quarter-note river plus hard volume terraces turns Q&A into theater: the question lives on one clear shelf of volume, the answer drops or jumps to another. No fades, no easing — the dynamic shift is the conversation.
steps: Pick two distinct volume levels before you start — loud for questions, soft for answers (or vice versa).
Sing the question in even quarters on the upper shelf, ending on an unresolved tone.
Snap immediately to the lower shelf for the answer, still in even quarters, descending to the root.
Never crossfade between levels; let the switch happen on the first note of the new phrase.
After a few pairs, flip the assignment so the answer is louder than the question and feel the difference.
examples: Q (loud): root → third → fifth → sixth (unresolved). A (soft): fifth → third → second → root.
Q (soft): second → third → fifth (rising, hovering). A (loud): fifth → fourth → second → root.
Q (loud): root → fourth → fifth → seventh (reaching). A (soft): sixth → fifth → third → root.
questionAnswer_river_terraces.watchOut: The trap here is sliding between volume levels instead of snapping. If you hear a crescendo between phrases, reset and make the shift happen on one single note.

questionAnswer_river_whisper.interaction: Whispered quarters with Q&A is intimate dialogue — two phrases murmured in a hushed room. The conversational structure has to come entirely from pitch shape and timing, because volume is a flat line near silence.
steps: Find your quietest sustainable voice and test that the drone still sits underneath it.
Sing even quarters, letting every note be clearly pitched but barely present.
Make questions rise and hang on an unresolved tone; make answers descend to the root.
Exaggerate the pitch contrast — questions reach higher, answers settle lower — since dynamics can't help.
Leave a full breath of silence between pairs so each new question feels like a fresh thought.
examples: Q: root → third → fifth → sixth (unresolved, whispered). A: fifth → fourth → second → root (whispered, home).
Q: second → fourth → fifth (hovering). A: fourth → third → second → root.
Q: root → fifth → sixth → seventh (reaching up). A: sixth → fifth → third → root (settling).
questionAnswer_river_whisper.watchOut: The common trap is the answer losing its identity and blending into the question because everything is whisper-flat — push the answer's pitch clearly downward so the ear still hears resolution.
questionAnswer_river_whisper.deeperInsight: Whispering forces you to articulate with pitch and phrasing alone, which trains a more precise melodic intention.

questionAnswer_river_accentMap.interaction: Quiet quarters with one loud note per bar turns Q&A into a game: where does the accent land? Drop it on the answer's resolution and the period is huge. Drop it on the question's peak and the question sounds insistent.
steps: Sing the question in soft even quarters, placing one punchy accent on its highest note.
Sing the answer in soft even quarters, placing the accent on the final home note.
Keep all non-accent notes at the same low volume — no creeping up.
Try pairs where the accent lands only on the answer's last note, so the question feels like a murmured ask.
Then try pairs where both phrases have accents and feel how the emphasis shifts the meaning.
examples: Q: root → third → [FIFTH] → sixth (accent on peak, unresolved). A: sixth → fourth → second → [ROOT] (accent on home).
Q: second → [FOURTH] → third → fifth (accent early, drifting up). A: fifth → third → second → [ROOT] (accent on resolution).
Q: root → [THIRD] → fifth → sixth (accent on third). A: fifth → [THIRD] → second → root (answering accent lower).
questionAnswer_river_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accents becoming predictable and robotic. Vary which beat the accent lands on between pairs so the conversation stays alive.

questionAnswer_river_forte.interaction: Full-voice quarters with Q&A is a bold public conversation — nothing hushed, nothing private. Pitch shape alone has to carry the question mark and the period, because dynamics stay pinned at the top.
steps: Support the breath — forte on even quarters is surprisingly taxing, so anchor with good posture.
Keep the quarter pulse unwavering as the pitch climbs through the question.
Let the answer be just as loud but clearly descending to the home note.
Leave a real silence between pairs or the whole thing turns into one continuous shout.
Exaggerate the pitch rise on questions and pitch fall on answers so the contour reads even at full volume.
examples: Q: root → third → fifth → sixth (rising, full voice). A: sixth → fifth → third → root (descending, full voice).
Q: root → fourth → fifth → seventh (reaching high). A: sixth → fifth → fourth → root (coming home).
Q: third → fifth → sixth (rising, unresolved). A: fifth → fourth → second → root (resolved).
questionAnswer_river_forte.watchOut: At full volume, pitch accuracy often slips on the high notes. If the question's peak is going sharp or flat, back off the volume slightly and prioritize the pitch.

questionAnswer_burst_swell.interaction: Burst rhythm is already built like Q&A — a flurry then silence — and adding swell makes each burst itself breathe. The question bursts in fading up, the answer bursts in at full and fades down into its own rest.
steps: Plan each burst as 4-6 quick notes followed by 2-3 beats of rest.
Shape the question's burst rising in pitch and swelling in volume, unresolved at the end of the burst.
Use the rest after the question as real breath, not a placeholder.
Shape the answer's burst descending to the root, starting full and fading into the trailing silence.
After each pair, wait a full extra beat before starting the next question.
examples: Q: root-third-fifth-sixth (burst, swelling up, unresolved) [rest]. A: sixth-fifth-third-root (burst, swelling down to silence) [rest].
Q: third-fourth-fifth-seventh (burst rising, fading in) [rest]. A: seventh-sixth-fifth-root (burst descending, fading out) [rest].
Q: root-fourth-fifth-sixth (burst up) [rest]. A: sixth-fourth-second-root (burst down, crests then fades).
questionAnswer_burst_swell.watchOut: The trap is the answer's burst sounding identical to the question's burst — if you can't tell them apart, make the answer clearly descend and clearly resolve on the root.
questionAnswer_burst_swell.deeperInsight: Swell plus burst makes each phrase feel like a wave landing on the beach — energy arrives, breaks, and recedes.

questionAnswer_burst_terraces.interaction: Two bursts on two different shelves of volume — the clearest possible Q&A. The silences between bursts give you time to reset to the new volume level cleanly before the next attack.
steps: Pick two volume shelves and commit to them before singing.
Fire the question burst on the upper shelf, 4-6 quick rising notes ending unresolved.
Use the rest after to physically reset your support for the lower shelf.
Fire the answer burst on the lower shelf, descending to the root.
Occasionally flip: soft question, loud answer, and notice how the meaning shifts.
examples: Q (loud): root-third-fifth-sixth (unresolved burst) [rest]. A (soft): sixth-fifth-third-root (resolved burst).
Q (soft): second-fourth-fifth (hovering burst) [rest]. A (loud): fifth-fourth-second-root (declarative resolution).
Q (loud): root-fifth-sixth-seventh (big reaching burst) [rest]. A (soft): sixth-fifth-third-root (quiet home).
questionAnswer_burst_terraces.watchOut: The trap is the volume shelves drifting closer together as you get tired. Re-anchor the two levels every few pairs by testing each one in isolation.

questionAnswer_burst_whisper.interaction: Whispered bursts are like urgent secrets — flurries of quiet notes followed by charged silence. The Q&A shape has to live in the pitch contour because the volume stays low and flat throughout.
steps: Find a whisper voice that still pitches cleanly and test it on a sustained note first.
Burst the question: 4-6 rising whispered notes, ending unresolved.
Let the silence after feel conspiratorial, not empty.
