Burst the answer: 4-6 whispered notes descending firmly to the root.
Keep the rhythm of the bursts tight — whisper doesn't mean sloppy timing.
examples: Q: root-third-fifth-sixth (whispered rising burst) [rest]. A: sixth-fourth-second-root (whispered descending burst).
Q: second-fourth-fifth-seventh (whispered reach) [rest]. A: sixth-fifth-third-root (whispered settle).
Q: root-fourth-fifth-sixth [rest]. A: fifth-third-second-root.
questionAnswer_burst_whisper.watchOut: The trap is the answer starting to rise because the whisper makes you lose track of direction — always aim the answer's first note downward from where the question ended.

questionAnswer_burst_accentMap.interaction: Quiet bursts with one hard accent per bar turns each phrase into a small explosion with a single bright spark. Place the answer's accent on its resolution and the period slams shut.
steps: Sing the question as a quiet burst with one punchy note in the middle on its peak.
Use the rest to prepare the answer.
Sing the answer as a quiet burst with the accent landing exactly on the final home note.
Keep the non-accent notes genuinely quiet — don't let them creep up toward the accent.
Experiment with placing the question's accent on its first note (declarative opening) versus its peak (hovering ask).
examples: Q: root-third-[FIFTH]-sixth (quiet burst, accent on peak) [rest]. A: sixth-fourth-second-[ROOT] (quiet burst, accent on home).
Q: [ROOT]-third-fifth-sixth (accent on opening) [rest]. A: sixth-fifth-third-[ROOT] (accent on resolution).
Q: second-[FOURTH]-fifth-sixth [rest]. A: fifth-fourth-second-[ROOT].
questionAnswer_burst_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accent becoming the only thing you can hear and the surrounding burst turning mushy. The quiet notes still need to be in time and in tune.

questionAnswer_burst_forte.interaction: Full-volume bursts with Q&A is dramatic call-and-response — two declarations with weight. The bursts are already well-shaped phrases, so forte just ensures the ceiling of the room hears every word.
steps: Support each burst with deep breath — 4-6 fast loud notes is athletic work.
Burst the question rising, unresolved, at full voice.
Use the rest to breathe fully, not shallowly.
Burst the answer descending to the root at full voice.
Make sure each burst has a clear shape — at forte, mushy contours are easy.
examples: Q: root-third-fifth-sixth (forte burst rising) [rest]. A: sixth-fifth-third-root (forte burst descending).
Q: third-fifth-sixth-seventh (reaching high) [rest]. A: seventh-fifth-third-root.
Q: root-fourth-fifth-sixth (forte rising) [rest]. A: fifth-fourth-second-root.
questionAnswer_burst_forte.watchOut: The trap at forte is going sharp at the top of the question and then the answer sounding equally loud rather than obviously resolving. Anchor the root in your body so the answer lands definitively.

questionAnswer_space_swell.interaction: Space rhythm with swell turns each note into a tiny event that arrives, peaks, and dissolves. Questions become reaching single tones that fade in unresolved; answers become descending tones that crest and return to silence.
steps: Choose just a few notes per phrase — space means at least half of every bar is rest.
Let each question note fade in from nothing, hang, and stay unresolved.
Let each answer note swell in, crest, and fade to silence on the home tone.
Breathe deeply in the rests — they are the point, not the gaps.
Resist the urge to fill the silence with extra notes.
examples: Q: [rest] third........ fifth........ (swelling in, unresolved). A: fifth........ third........ root........ (swelling and fading home).
Q: fourth........ sixth........ (reaching, fading in). A: fifth........ second........ root........ (descending, fading out).
Q: third........ seventh........ (hovering). A: sixth........ third........ root........
questionAnswer_space_swell.watchOut: The trap is filling the rests because they feel too long — trust the silence. If you catch yourself crowding notes in, consciously wait an extra beat.
questionAnswer_space_swell.deeperInsight: Space plus swell is the closest improvisation gets to meditation — each pair is a complete thought with its own birth, peak, and death.

questionAnswer_space_terraces.interaction: Sparse notes on two different volume shelves make Q&A feel like two distant speakers across a canyon. The silences between notes give you time to reset to the new shelf without any audible fade.
steps: Pick two clear volume shelves.
Drop sparse question notes on the upper shelf, leaving long rests between them.
End the question on an unresolved tone, then wait at least a full beat.
Drop sparse answer notes on the lower shelf, ending on the root.
Do not ease between shelves — snap to the new level on the first note of each phrase.
examples: Q (loud): third........ fifth........ sixth........ (unresolved). A (soft): fifth........ third........ root........
Q (soft): fourth........ seventh........ (hovering high). A (loud): sixth........ third........ root........
Q (loud): root........ fifth........ sixth........ A (soft): fifth........ second........ root........
questionAnswer_space_terraces.watchOut: The trap is the two shelves not being distinct enough when there are so few notes to compare. Make the volume difference exaggerated — bigger than feels natural.

questionAnswer_space_whisper.interaction: Whispered sparse notes with Q&A is the quietest corner of the practice room — tiny barely-there tones placed in long silences. The pitch contour has to do all the work because volume is a flat line near zero.
steps: Test your quietest sustainable voice on one pitched note at the start.
Drop question notes in wide silences, each clearly pitched, each hanging unresolved.
Let the rest after the question be long — a full bar of silence is fine.
Drop answer notes descending toward the root, each just as quiet as the question.
Use the body to support the pitch even at whisper volume.
examples: Q: third........ sixth........ (whispered, unresolved). A: fifth........ second........ root........
Q: fourth........ seventh........ (whispered reach). A: sixth........ third........ root........
questionAnswer_space_whisper.watchOut: The trap is notes going flat at whisper volume because support drops — keep the body engaged. Also, the answer can easily sound identical to the question if you don't aim the pitch clearly downward.

questionAnswer_space_accentMap.interaction: Sparse quiet notes with a single accent per bar reduces Q&A to its essence — a handful of tones, one loud punctuation mark. Put the accent on the answer's final root and the period is carved in stone.
steps: Drop a few soft question notes, placing the accent on the highest or most unresolved one.
Let the silence after feel heavy.
Drop a few soft answer notes descending to the root, placing the accent on the final home note.
Keep every non-accent note truly soft — in space rhythm they stand out if you let them creep up.
Sometimes skip the question's accent entirely so only the answer has weight.
examples: Q: third........ [FIFTH]........ (unresolved, peak accented). A: fourth........ second........ [ROOT].
Q: fourth........ seventh........ (no accent) A: fifth........ third........ [ROOT].
questionAnswer_space_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accent note overshadowing the pitch contour. The shape still needs to read as rising-unresolved then descending-resolved.

questionAnswer_space_forte.interaction: Forte sparse notes with Q&A is sculptural — a few big tones dropped into long silences. Every note is a bronze bell. The conversation unfolds slowly at full volume.
steps: Support each note completely — forte single tones are exposed.
Drop question notes at full voice, rising or hovering unresolved, with wide rests between.
Let the rest after the question ring out — don't rush the answer.
Drop answer notes descending firmly to the root at full voice.
Keep intonation rock-solid — there's nowhere to hide at this volume and spacing.
examples: Q: third........ seventh........ (forte, unresolved). A: sixth........ third........ root........
Q: root........ fifth........ sixth........ (forte reaching). A: fifth........ third........ root........
Q: fourth........ sixth........ A: fifth........ second........ root........
questionAnswer_space_forte.watchOut: The trap at forte space is notes landing slightly out of tune and the silence exposing it ruthlessly. Slow down and really place each pitch.
questionAnswer_space_forte.deeperInsight: Space plus forte teaches you what a single note actually sounds like. Most singers rush through notes they can't survive on their own.

questionAnswer_offbeat_swell.interaction: Offbeat notes with swell and Q&A is sly and floating — the rhythm already feels suspended by avoiding the downbeats, and the swell makes each offbeat note appear and disappear like a ghost. The Q&A contour gives it direction.
steps: Lock onto the metronome's downbeats mentally so you can avoid them accurately.
Place question notes on the 'and' of beats, fading in and rising, ending unresolved.
Place answer notes on the 'and' of beats, swelling and descending to the root.
Keep the body relaxed — offbeat phrasing tightens people up.
Let the silences align with the downbeats so the syncopation stays audible.
examples: Q: (&)third (&)fifth (&)sixth (rising offbeats, fading in). A: (&)sixth (&)fourth (&)second (&)root (swelling home).
Q: (&)fourth (&)seventh (unresolved offbeats). A: (&)sixth (&)third (&)root.
Q: (&)root (&)fifth (&)sixth A: (&)fifth (&)third (&)root.
questionAnswer_offbeat_swell.watchOut: The trap is notes drifting onto the downbeats as the swell distracts you — the moment you hear yourself land on 1 or 3, reset the groove.

questionAnswer_offbeat_terraces.interaction: Offbeat Q&A on two terraces of volume is playful reggae-style dialogue — one voice on the loud shelf comments on the offbeats, another voice answers from the quiet shelf on its own offbeats. The downbeat is a shared silence both phrases honor.
steps: Set two clear volume shelves.
Sing the question on the offbeats at the upper shelf, unresolved.
Snap to the lower shelf for the answer, still on offbeats, descending to the root.
Never let notes drift to the downbeats — the syncopation is the whole container.
Try swapping shelves: soft unresolved question, loud resolved answer, and feel the shift.
examples: Q (loud): (&)third (&)fifth (&)sixth (unresolved offbeats). A (soft): (&)sixth (&)fourth (&)root.
Q (soft): (&)fourth (&)seventh. A (loud): (&)sixth (&)third (&)root.
Q (loud): (&)root (&)fifth (&)sixth A (soft): (&)fifth (&)second (&)root.
questionAnswer_offbeat_terraces.watchOut: The trap is the volume shift dragging the timing slightly onto the downbeats. Stay loose in the body and trust the click.

questionAnswer_offbeat_whisper.interaction: Whispered offbeat Q&A is like secret code tapped between downbeats — two phrases of quiet upbeats in a hushed room. The conversation happens in the cracks of the rhythm.
steps: Feel the downbeats in your body without voicing them.
Whisper question notes only on the 'ands,' rising and unresolved.
Whisper answer notes only on the 'ands,' descending to the root.
Keep the pitch crisp even at whisper volume — support from the core.
Leave a clear rest between pairs so the conversation has space to breathe.
examples: Q: (&)third (&)fifth (&)sixth (whispered, unresolved). A: (&)sixth (&)fourth (&)root.
Q: (&)fourth (&)seventh (whispered hover). A: (&)sixth (&)third (&)root.
Q: (&)root (&)fifth (&)sixth A: (&)fifth (&)third (&)root.
questionAnswer_offbeat_whisper.watchOut: The trap is timing getting loose because whisper makes you internally relax — the downbeats still need to be felt sharply even though you're not landing on them.

questionAnswer_offbeat_accentMap.interaction: Quiet offbeats with a loud accent per bar and Q&A is reggae meets conversation — the accent becomes a single snapped handclap punctuating each phrase. Put the answer's accent on its last offbeat resolution and the period is snapped shut.
steps: Sing soft question offbeats with one loud accent somewhere in the phrase.
Keep the accent itself on an offbeat — don't cheat it onto a downbeat for ease.
Sing soft answer offbeats descending, with the accent landing on the final root (which is also on an 'and').
Keep the non-accent notes truly soft so the one loud note punches.
Experiment with the accent landing early in the question (declarative) vs. late (hanging).
examples: Q: (&)third (&)[FIFTH] (&)sixth (accent on peak, unresolved). A: (&)sixth (&)third (&)[ROOT] (accent on resolution).
Q: (&)[ROOT] (&)fifth (&)sixth A: (&)fifth (&)third (&)[ROOT].
Q: (&)fourth (&)seventh A: (&)sixth (&)[THIRD] (&)root.
questionAnswer_offbeat_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accent pulling itself onto a downbeat without you noticing. Record a short take and verify — if the accent landed on 1 or 3, try again.

questionAnswer_offbeat_forte.interaction: Full-volume offbeat Q&A is a bold, confident reggae-style conversation — no hiding, all upbeats, loud on both phrases. The challenge is keeping the syncopation clean when the body wants to slam on downbeats.
steps: Breathe deeply and support fully — forte offbeats are athletic.
Sing question notes at full voice on the 'ands,' rising and unresolved.
Sing answer notes at full voice on the 'ands,' descending to the root.
Mentally ghost the downbeats so they stay silent.
Take a clear breath between pairs — no rushing.
examples: Q: (&)root (&)third (&)fifth (&)sixth (forte, rising). A: (&)sixth (&)fifth (&)third (&)root.
Q: (&)fourth (&)sixth (&)seventh (forte hover). A: (&)sixth (&)third (&)root.
Q: (&)third (&)fifth (&)sixth A: (&)fifth (&)second (&)root.
questionAnswer_offbeat_forte.watchOut: The trap at forte is the volume shoving notes early onto the downbeat. If you catch a note landing on 1, immediately slow down and reset.

questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_swell.interaction: A repeating rhythmic cell with swell and Q&A turns each phrase into a recognizable chant that swells in and out. The seed repeats identically for both question and answer — only the pitch direction and dynamic shape distinguish them.
steps: Choose one short rhythmic cell (e.g., short-short-long, or dotted-quarter + eighth) and commit to it.
Use that exact cell for the question, with pitches rising and volume fading in, ending unresolved.
Use the same cell for the answer, with pitches descending to the root and volume swelling and fading.
Let the pitches change freely round to round but keep the rhythm identical.
Breathe between pairs so each round of the cell feels fresh.
examples: Cell: short-short-long. Q: third-fifth-sixth~~~ (swelling in, unresolved). A: sixth-fourth-root~~~ (cresting and fading).
Cell: long-short-short. Q: fifth~~~ sixth seventh (rising, fading in). A: sixth~~~ third root (resolving).
Cell: short-short-short-long. Q: root-third-fifth-sixth~~~ A: sixth-fifth-third-root~~~
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_swell.watchOut: The trap is the rhythmic cell drifting from phrase to phrase — when that happens, the answer starts feeling like a new idea instead of a parallel response. Lock the cell in your body first.

questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_terraces.interaction: Repeated rhythmic cell on two volume terraces is one of the clearest Q&A shapes possible — same rhythm, two clearly different volumes, one obvious resolution. It sounds like a theme stated at one dynamic and answered at another.
steps: Pick your cell and your two volume shelves.
State the cell as the question on the upper shelf, pitches rising, unresolved.
State the same cell as the answer on the lower shelf, pitches descending to the root.
Repeat several pairs keeping the cell identical.
Flip the shelves occasionally to hear soft-question, loud-answer dialogue.
examples: Cell: ta-ti-ta. Q (loud): third-fifth-sixth (unresolved). A (soft): fifth-third-root.
Cell: ti-ti-ta. Q (soft): fourth-fifth-seventh. A (loud): sixth-third-root.
Cell: ta-ta-ti-ti. Q (loud): root-third-fifth-sixth A (soft): sixth-fifth-third-root.
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_terraces.watchOut: The trap is the answer changing the rhythm slightly because the quieter shelf makes you rush or drag. Metronome it strictly.
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_terraces.deeperInsight: This combo is actually how classical theme-and-variation starts — same rhythm, different dynamic, different pitch contour.

questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_whisper.interaction: A whispered repeated cell with Q&A is like a mantra with two alternating meanings — same shape, same hush, but one version rises and hangs, the other falls and settles. Intimate and hypnotic.
steps: Choose a clear cell and establish it silently in your body before singing.
Whisper the cell as the question, pitches rising, unresolved.
Whisper the cell as the answer, pitches descending to the root.
Let the pitches vary round to round while the cell stays frozen.
Let the silences between cells feel contemplative.
examples: Cell: short-short-long. Q: third-fifth-sixth~~~ (whispered, unresolved). A: fifth-third-root~~~ (whispered, home).
Cell: ti-ti-ti-ti. Q: fourth-fifth-sixth-seventh. A: sixth-fifth-third-root.
Cell: long-ti-ti. Q: fifth~~~ sixth seventh A: sixth~~~ third root.
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_whisper.watchOut: The trap is pitch direction collapsing at whisper volume and the answer sounding identical to the question. Exaggerate the pitch drop on the answer.

questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_accentMap.interaction: A repeated quiet cell with one accent per bar, shaped as Q&A, turns each cell into a miniature declaration. Put the accent on the same relative position in both phrases and you get beautiful parallelism; move it and you get a rhetorical twist.
steps: Pick a cell and decide where the accent lives in it (first note? last note? middle?).
Sing the question cell quietly with the accent placed on the chosen position; pitches rise, end unresolved.
Sing the answer cell quietly with the accent on the same position or moved to the final root; pitches descend.
Keep the non-accent notes genuinely soft so the one loud moment punches.
Record yourself occasionally to verify the accent is landing where you think it is.
examples: Cell: ti-ti-ta with accent on the 'ta.' Q: third-fifth-[SIXTH] (unresolved). A: fifth-third-[ROOT].
Cell: ta-ti-ti. Q: [FIFTH]-sixth-seventh A: [FIFTH]-third-root.
Cell: ti-ti-ti-ta. Q: root-third-fifth-[SIXTH] A: sixth-fifth-third-[ROOT].
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accent drifting to whatever note feels easiest, breaking the parallel structure. Commit to where in the cell the accent lives.

questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_forte.interaction: A repeated cell at full volume shaped as Q&A is pure chant energy — a clear rhythmic motto belted out twice, once rising, once resolving. It teaches you to build identity through rhythm alone.
steps: Support each cell fully — forte repetition is exhausting.
State the cell as the question at full voice, pitches rising, unresolved.
State the same cell as the answer at full voice, pitches descending to the root.
Keep the rhythm absolutely identical across pairs — no creative variation on the cell itself.
Breathe fully between pairs or the whole thing turns into one long shout.
examples: Cell: short-short-long. Q: third-fifth-sixth~~~ (forte unresolved). A: fifth-third-root~~~ (forte home).
Cell: ti-ti-ta. Q: fourth-sixth-seventh A: sixth-third-root.
Cell: ta-ti-ti-ta. Q: root~~~ third-fifth-sixth~~~ A: sixth~~~ fifth-third-root~~~
questionAnswer_rhythmSeed_forte.watchOut: The trap at forte is pitch accuracy slipping on the high question notes. Prioritize pitch over volume if you have to choose.

questionAnswer_triplets_swell.interaction: Triplet flow with swell and Q&A is rolling and liquid — three notes per beat gives the phrases an elastic feel, and the swell makes each triplet group breathe. The Q&A arc gives the rolling motion a purpose.
steps: Internalize the triplet pulse before singing — three even notes per beat.
Roll the question in triplets rising, fading in, ending on an unresolved tone.
Roll the answer in triplets descending to the root, swelling and fading.
Let the triplets stay liquid — no accenting the downbeat of each group.
Breathe between pairs to let the swell actually return to silence.
examples: Q: (root-third-fifth)(sixth-fifth-seventh) (rising triplets, fading in, unresolved). A: (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-root) (swelling down to home).
Q: (third-fourth-fifth)(sixth-seventh-sixth) A: (fifth-fourth-third)(second-second-root).
Q: (root-second-third)(fifth-sixth-seventh) A: (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-root).
questionAnswer_triplets_swell.watchOut: The trap is the triplets turning into uneven bursts because the swell distracts you from subdivision. Keep the triplet pulse audible in the body even while volume moves.

questionAnswer_triplets_terraces.interaction: Triplets on two volume terraces shaped as Q&A is rolling conversation — one speaker rolls out triplets at one shelf, the other responds in triplets at another. The triplet flow connects the phrases while the volume shift separates them.
steps: Pick two clear volume shelves.
Roll the question in triplets on the upper shelf, rising, unresolved.
Snap to the lower shelf and roll the answer in triplets descending to the root.
Keep the triplets even — no rushing at the volume switch.
Try swapping shelves occasionally.
examples: Q (loud): (third-fifth-sixth)(fifth-sixth-seventh) (rising). A (soft): (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-root).
Q (soft): (fourth-fifth-sixth)(seventh-sixth-seventh) A (loud): (sixth-fourth-third)(second-second-root).
Q (loud): (root-third-fifth)(sixth-sixth-seventh) A (soft): (fifth-fourth-third)(second-root-root).
questionAnswer_triplets_terraces.watchOut: The trap is the triplets drifting into duplets at the quieter shelf. Keep subdividing three per beat in your head even when the volume drops.

questionAnswer_triplets_whisper.interaction: Whispered triplet Q&A is a rushing stream heard from far away — all the rolling motion is there, but the volume keeps it tiny. The Q&A shape comes entirely from pitch rise and fall inside the triplet flow.
steps: Find a whisper voice that still articulates triplets cleanly.
Whisper triplets rising for the question, ending unresolved.
Whisper triplets descending to the root for the answer.
Keep the triplet subdivision crisp — whisper makes people smear things.
Silence between pairs should feel like the stream momentarily pausing.
examples: Q: (third-fourth-fifth)(sixth-seventh-sixth) whispered rising. A: (fifth-fourth-third)(second-root-root).
Q: (root-second-third)(fifth-sixth-seventh) A: (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-root).
Q: (fourth-fifth-sixth)(seventh-sixth-seventh) A: (sixth-fourth-third)(second-root-root).
questionAnswer_triplets_whisper.watchOut: The trap is the triplet rhythm degrading into mushy fast notes at whisper volume. Tap your foot or wiggle a finger in triplets to anchor the pulse physically.

questionAnswer_triplets_accentMap.interaction: Quiet triplet flow with one loud accent per bar, shaped as Q&A, creates a rolling stream with an occasional splash. Land the accent on the downbeat of a triplet group and the rhythm stays clear; land it mid-group and everything lurches thrillingly.
steps: Roll the question in soft triplets with one punchy accent on the highest note.
Keep non-accent triplets genuinely quiet.
Roll the answer in soft triplets with the accent landing on the resolving root.
Try both on-beat and off-beat accent placements within the triplet.
Vary where the accent falls between pairs to keep things alive.
examples: Q: (third-fifth-[SIXTH])(seventh-sixth-seventh) (accent on peak, unresolved). A: (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-[ROOT]).
Q: (root-third-fifth)([SIXTH]-seventh-sixth) A: (fifth-fourth-third)(second-root-[ROOT]).
Q: ([ROOT]-third-fifth)(sixth-seventh-sixth) A: (sixth-fifth-third)(second-[ROOT]-root).
questionAnswer_triplets_accentMap.watchOut: The trap is the accent dragging the triplets out of subdivision around it. Practice the cell quietly first, then add the accent without disturbing the pulse.

questionAnswer_triplets_forte.interaction: Full-voice triplet Q&A is gospel or flamenco energy — rolling declarations at full power, once rising, once resolving. The physical demand is significant; the payoff is thrilling.
steps: Breathe deeply — forte triplets burn through air.
Roll the question in triplets at full voice, rising, unresolved.
Roll the answer in triplets at full voice, descending to the root.
Keep the triplet subdivision unshakably even.
Breathe fully between pairs.
examples: Q: (root-third-fifth)(sixth-seventh-sixth) (forte rising). A: (sixth-fifth-fourth)(third-second-root).
Q: (third-fourth-fifth)(seventh-sixth-seventh) A: (sixth-fifth-third)(second-root-root).
Q: (fourth-fifth-sixth)(seventh-sixth-seventh) A: (sixth-fourth-third)(second-second-root).
questionAnswer_triplets_forte.watchOut: The trap at forte triplets is the rhythm collapsing into duplets as you tire. Keep the triplet pulse felt physically, even between pairs.
questionAnswer_triplets_forte.deeperInsight: Forte triplets with Q&A is one of the oldest vocal shapes in world music — it's in gospel shouts, flamenco cante, and Qawwali. You're tapping into something ancient.

minor-pentatonic.name: Min Pent
minor-pentatonic.character: Wistful and bluesy with five notes that all feel safe over a minor chord — the universal improviser's playground from Delta blues to rock solos to Japanese folk melodies. There are no wrong notes, so this is where you practice phrasing, bends, and breath instead of worrying about theory.
minor-pentatonic.watchPoint: Lean into the flat 3rd scale degree with a slow bend or a held vibrato — that's the note that turns a run into a cry.

major-pentatonic.name: Maj Pent
major-pentatonic.character: Bright, open, and tension-free — the sound of campfire songs, country solos, Appalachian fiddle, and East Asian folk melodies. Every note rings consonant against a major chord, so phrases come out sounding joyful and settled almost by default.
major-pentatonic.watchPoint: Circle back to the 6th scale degree — it's the sweetest color in the scale and the note that keeps major pentatonic from sounding like a nursery rhyme.

blues.name: Blues
blues.character: The minor pentatonic plus one dangerous extra note — the flat 5th 'blue note' that gives the scale its cry. This is the language of T-Bone Walker, B.B. King, and Muddy Waters: raw, vocal, and built around bends and slides more than runs.
blues.watchPoint: Slide through the flat 5th scale degree rather than landing on it — it's a passing scream between the 4th and 5th, not a resting place.

major.name: Major
major.character: The foundational sound of Western music — bright, fully resolved, and instantly familiar from Beatles songs, church hymns, and classical melodies. Every scale degree has a clear job, which makes it the best scale for learning how tension and release actually work.
major.watchPoint: The 7th scale degree is a half-step magnet pulling up to the root — leaning on it creates the 'almost home' feeling that defines major-key melodies.

natural-minor.name: Minor
natural-minor.character: Melancholy and introspective — the sound of folk ballads, film noir soundtracks, and Leonard Cohen verses. It's the relative minor of the major scale, but darker and more grounded because the half-step pull toward the root is gone.
natural-minor.watchPoint: The flat 6th scale degree is the scale's heartbreak note — hold it over the root and you'll hear why minor keys feel like longing.

dorian.name: Dorian
dorian.character: A minor scale with one crucial brightening — the raised 6th that makes it feel jazzier and more hopeful than natural minor. This is the sound of Miles Davis's 'So What', Santana's modal jams, funk vamps, and Celtic reels: minor but not sad.
dorian.watchPoint: Target the raised 6th scale degree over a minor chord — it's the one note that separates Dorian from regular minor and gives the scale its cool, floating quality.

mixolydian.name: Mixolydian
mixolydian.character: The major scale with a flat 7th — laid-back, unresolved, and perfect for jamming over a single chord forever. It's the sound of Allman Brothers jams, Grateful Dead grooves, Irish jigs, and modal rock that never wants to go home.
mixolydian.watchPoint: Lean into the flat 7th scale degree instead of resolving up to the root — that's the 'loose' note that keeps Mixolydian from collapsing back into a plain major scale.

phrygian.name: Phrygian
phrygian.character: A minor scale with a flat 2nd — dark, Spanish, and instantly exotic because the note right above the root is only a half-step away. You hear it in flamenco, metal riffs, and anything that wants a Middle Eastern or menacing flavor.
phrygian.watchPoint: Hammer the half-step above the root — that flat 2nd scale degree is the whole personality of the scale, and squeezing it against the root is what makes Phrygian sound like Phrygian.
